title: Best VPN for Linux in 2026: Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora and Arch Tested
description: Most VPNs treat Linux as an afterthought. We tested the ones that actually work — with GUI apps, WireGuard support, and kill switches that hold.
content:
Linux users get a raw deal from most VPN companies. You're handed a command-line tool with a 12-step setup guide and told to be grateful. The mainstream VPN market is built for Windows and macOS. Linux is the footnote.

But the landscape has quietly improved. A few providers have built real Linux apps with graphical interfaces, and WireGuard's inclusion in the Linux kernel since version 5.6 means kernel-native VPN tunneling is now standard. This guide covers the best VPNs that actually respect Linux, explains how to set them up via CLI when needed, and walks through kill switches and split tunneling without third-party apps.

## Why Most VPNs Fail on Linux

The problem isn't technical. It's commercial. Linux users represent a small fraction of the consumer VPN market, so most providers invest in Windows and macOS apps and port the bare minimum to Linux.

What "Linux support" usually means in practice:

- A generic OpenVPN config file you have to import manually into NetworkManager
- A CLI tool that works but requires you to remember flags and connection names
- No GUI, no status indicator, no kill switch in the app
- Packages that haven't been updated since the provider last thought about Linux

This matters because a VPN with no kill switch on Linux will leak your real IP whenever the tunnel drops. On a platform where most users value privacy, that's a significant failure.

## Mullvad: The Best Linux VPN in 2026

Mullvad builds the most complete Linux experience of any major VPN provider. They offer a full graphical application — not a web wrapper, not Electron, but a native app — available as a Flatpak (works on any distro), an apt package for Ubuntu and Debian, an rpm package for Fedora and RHEL, and an AUR package for Arch.

**What the Mullvad Linux app includes:**

- A GUI with server selection, protocol switching, and connection status
- WireGuard and OpenVPN support, switchable in the settings panel
- A kill switch that activates by default and blocks all traffic if the tunnel drops
- Split tunneling via app name or IP range
- DAITA (defense against AI traffic analysis) — Mullvad's own obfuscation layer
- Multihop routing (chain two servers across different countries)

To install on Ubuntu or Debian:

```bash
sudo apt install curl
curl -fsSL https://repository.mullvad.net/deb/mullvad-keyring.asc | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/mullvad-keyring.gpg
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/mullvad-keyring.gpg] https://repository.mullvad.net/deb/stable $(lsb_release -cs) main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/mullvad.list
sudo apt update && sudo apt install mullvad-vpn
```

Mullvad costs €5 per month with no annual discount, no accounts, and no email required at signup. You pay with an account number. That's the most privacy-respecting billing model in the industry.

## ProtonVPN: The Open Source Option With a Real GUI

ProtonVPN publishes the source code for all its apps, including Linux, on GitHub. You can audit exactly what the app does. That's rare. Most providers claim to be open source and mean "we published the encryption library."

The ProtonVPN Linux app offers a full GUI with a similar feature set to Mullvad: server selection, protocol toggle, kill switch, split tunneling. It's available as a deb package, rpm, Flatpak, and snap.

To install on Ubuntu:

```bash
wget https://repo.protonvpn.com/debian/dists/stable/main/binary-all/protonvpn-stable-release_1.0.3-3_all.deb
sudo dpkg -i protonvpn-stable-release_1.0.3-3_all.deb
sudo apt update && sudo apt install proton-vpn-gnome-desktop
```

ProtonVPN also offers a genuinely usable free tier with no logs and no data selling. Free tier users get access to servers in three countries at reduced speeds, which is acceptable for basic privacy use.

The main consideration is price. ProtonVPN's paid plans start at $9.99 per month, or $4.99 per month on the annual plan. That's more expensive than Mullvad. If budget matters and you want the best Linux support, Mullvad wins on price.

## NordVPN: Strong CLI, No GUI

NordVPN provides a polished Linux CLI client called `nordvpn` that covers most use cases. There's no graphical app, but the CLI is genuinely well-documented and the commands are intuitive.

Installation on Ubuntu or Debian:

```bash
sh <(curl -sSf https://downloads.nordcdn.com/apps/linux/install.sh)
```

After installation, the key commands:

```bash
nordvpn login          # opens browser for authentication
nordvpn connect        # connects to the fastest available server
nordvpn connect DE     # connects to Germany
nordvpn set killswitch on
nordvpn set cybersec on  # blocks ads and malware at DNS level
nordvpn status
nordvpn disconnect
```

NordVPN uses NordLynx, its WireGuard-based protocol, by default on Linux. WireGuard tunneling with NordVPN is fast and stable. The kill switch works at the network level, not just the app level, so it survives crashes.

NordVPN costs $3.99 per month on the two-year plan. For users who are comfortable with CLI tools, it's a cost-effective option with a large server network (6,300+ servers in 111 countries).

## WireGuard vs OpenVPN on Linux: Always Choose WireGuard

WireGuard has been part of the Linux kernel since version 5.6 (April 2020). Ubuntu 20.04 LTS, Debian 11, Fedora 36, and all later releases include it natively. You don't need to install anything to use WireGuard tunneling — it's already there.

**Why WireGuard is better on Linux:**

- OpenVPN runs in userspace. WireGuard runs in the kernel. Kernel-level code is faster and more stable.
- WireGuard's codebase is around 4,000 lines. OpenVPN's is over 100,000. Smaller code surface means fewer bugs.
- Connection establishment takes milliseconds with WireGuard vs several seconds with OpenVPN.
- WireGuard handles network changes (switching from WiFi to Ethernet, or changing IP addresses) more gracefully. OpenVPN often requires a reconnect.

The only reason to use OpenVPN on Linux is compatibility with older VPN providers that haven't added WireGuard support yet. If your VPN supports both, pick WireGuard.

## Setting Up a Kill Switch With iptables

If you're using a VPN without a native kill switch, or if you want an independent kill switch that doesn't rely on the VPN app, iptables gives you one at the OS level.

This rule set drops all traffic except through the WireGuard interface (replace `wg0` with your interface name, and `your.vpn.server.ip` with the actual server IP):

```bash
# Allow loopback and established connections
iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

# Allow traffic to the VPN server itself (so the tunnel can connect)
iptables -A OUTPUT -d your.vpn.server.ip -j ACCEPT

# Allow all traffic through the VPN tunnel
iptables -A OUTPUT -o wg0 -j ACCEPT

# Block everything else
iptables -A OUTPUT -j DROP
```

To make this persistent across reboots, install `iptables-persistent` and save the rules:

```bash
sudo apt install iptables-persistent
sudo netfilter-persistent save
```

## Split Tunneling on Linux

Split tunneling routes specific apps through the VPN while others use your regular connection. On Linux, this works via network namespaces and cgroups, though it's easier through VPN apps that support it natively.

Mullvad and ProtonVPN both support split tunneling in their Linux apps. You specify which applications bypass the VPN by name. NordVPN also supports split tunneling via its CLI:

```bash
nordvpn set splittunneling on
nordvpn whitelist add subnet 192.168.1.0/24  # route local network outside VPN
```

For manual split tunneling without an app, create a separate network namespace:

```bash
# Create a namespace that routes outside the VPN
ip netns add novpn
ip netns exec novpn ip link set lo up
# Then run apps in the namespace:
ip netns exec novpn firefox
```

This approach requires some networking knowledge but works on any Linux distribution regardless of the VPN provider.

## Which Distros Are Supported?

Here's the current support status for the three recommended VPNs:

| Distribution | Mullvad | ProtonVPN | NordVPN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ubuntu 20.04+ | Full GUI | Full GUI | CLI |
| Debian 10+ | Full GUI | Full GUI | CLI |
| Fedora 36+ | Full GUI | Full GUI | CLI |
| Arch Linux | AUR (GUI) | AUR | CLI |
| Linux Mint | Full GUI | Full GUI | CLI |
| Pop!_OS | Full GUI | Full GUI | CLI |
| openSUSE | Flatpak | Flatpak | Not official |

All three also distribute Flatpak packages, which work on any distro that runs Flatpak. If your distro isn't in the table, Flatpak is your safest install path.

## The Bottom Line

If you want the most complete Linux VPN experience, Mullvad is the answer. It's the only major provider that treats Linux as a first-class platform, not an afterthought. The GUI works well, WireGuard is the default protocol, and the kill switch is on by default.

ProtonVPN is the right choice if you want open source code you can audit, or if you need the free tier. NordVPN works well if you're comfortable with CLI tools and want a larger server network at a lower price.

Whatever you choose, use WireGuard, enable the kill switch, and verify your IP hasn't leaked after connecting (check via a site like ipleak.net before you rely on the VPN for anything sensitive).
